summary: Jeff Atwood explores Windows Vista's new search functionality, which replaces XP's familiar search syntax with Windows Desktop Search query syntax. He walks through the key differences, including the requirement for all-caps boolean operators and the distinction between indexed and non-indexed locations. The post highlights useful features like the Search Pane for learning syntax, the ability to search from the Start menu, and important caveats about file extension indexing. Atwood is generally positive about the upgrade, calling it a 'far richer search paradigm' than XP offered.
key_points: Vista's search syntax is completely different from XP, requiring users to relearn their search habits (e.g., boolean operators must be ALL CAPS)
The Search Pane serves as 'training wheels' that shows equivalent query terms as you use the GUI, helping users learn the new syntax
Search only extends to file contents in indexed locations, with a limited default set of indexed folders
The full-text indexer only indexes file extensions it recognizes, which users can modify in Advanced Options
Start menu search is highlighted as the 'coolest new feature' - just hit Windows key and start typing
money_quotes: Note that the boolean operator must be in all-caps to work. That was painful to figure out.
It's a far richer search paradigm than we ever had in XP.
Once you've learned the new Vista search syntax, you won't need the Search Pane training wheels any more; you can press CTRL+E and type in what you want. It's Google-icious.
I'm also very glad to see search now ignores compressed files by default. This was a real pain in XP, which insisted on digging through 600 megabyte ZIP files as a part of any search.
There's nothing to install, nothing to configure, searching just works in Vista. It's about time.
key_insight: Vista's search represents a significant upgrade over XP, but requires users to completely relearn their search habits and understand the distinction between indexed and non-indexed locations to use it effectively.
